summary: This guest post by Tom Adams argues that the Volcker Rule's primary purpose and measure of effectiveness should be preventing another Citigroup-style bailout. Citigroup required the largest government bailout in history in 2008, primarily due to its massive holdings of ABS CDOs that it accumulated to keep its lucrative securitization business running. When external buyers for super-senior CDO tranches dried up, Citi simply held them as 'investments' requiring minimal capital due to AAA ratings. The post contends that had Volcker Rule-style fair market accounting been required earlier, Citi would have hit capacity problems sooner and potentially cooled the mortgage market before the crisis.
key_points: Citigroup's bailout was the largest in government history, primarily caused by $150 billion in super-senior CDO holdings that became nearly worthless
Citi kept its lucrative CDO business running by buying its own super-senior tranches when external buyers dried up, exploiting AAA ratings to minimize capital requirements
The Volcker Rule's requirement to move covered assets to fair market valuation would have forced earlier recognition of CDO problems
Zions Bank's immediate impairment charge under the new rule demonstrates its potential effectiveness
Citi's CDO portfolio had massive leverage - $150 billion in CDOs was theoretically backed by $1.5 trillion in mortgage loans
money_quotes: The real purpose of the Volcker Rule is to prevent another Citigroup bailout and therefore the measure of its effectiveness is whether the rule would accomplish this.
Undeterred, Citi began to take down the super-senior bonds from the deals they were selling and holding them as 'investments' which required very little capital because they were AAA.
This approach enabled Citi to continue the very lucrative business of selling CDOs (to themselves!).
By any decent measure, Citi should have been broken up after this fiasco.
Had Citi been forced to do this as they acquired their CDOs, there is a decent chance they would have run into CDO capacity problems much sooner.
They might have had to justify their valuations with actual data rather than self-serving models.
key_insight: The Volcker Rule's true test is whether it prevents banks from accumulating toxic assets under favorable accounting treatment while the music plays, forcing them to face market reality before bailouts become necessary.
